title: Allez Vous

jokes.text: Mom screaming 'What?!' from another room in response to being called
jokes.setup: David politely asks his mother to come to him
jokes.punchline: Moira responds with the exact same demand — 'You come here!' — refusing to move despite the urgency
jokes.explanation: A quick, efficient character beat that immediately establishes Moira's imperious nature — she won't be inconvenienced even for a supposedly exciting delivery. The mirrored shouting structure is a clean comedic exchange, and it's very on-brand for Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, it's my dear friend reaching out. We're so close, you know. They mean everything to me.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Though I should mention, they're an angry drunk. But that doesn't change how much I care about them.

jokes.text: She can be an angry drunk, but we were always close.
jokes.setup: Moira is touched that her dear friend Justine has reached out to her in a time of need
jokes.punchline: She casually concedes that Justine 'can be an angry drunk' as a throwaway qualifier before doubling down on their closeness
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed Moira line. The dismissive qualifier 'she can be an angry drunk' is delivered as barely a consideration, which tells us everything about Moira's social world. The juxtaposition of a serious flaw being waved away reveals the absurdity of her friendships. Very economical and very character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, how thoughtful! A cosmetics starter kit from my dear friend.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A *starter* kit? What exactly are you suggesting? That my beauty regimen is somehow... insufficient? That I need to *start* over? How dare you insinuate that my complexion requires remedial intervention, you absolute charlatan. Take your pedestrian drugstore drivel and your thinly veiled insults elsewhere!

jokes.text: That frigid whore!
jokes.setup: Moira reads aloud that the gift is a 'Congratulations on your Allez Vous Starter Kit'
jokes.punchline: Moira's instant, explosive outrage — 'That frigid whore!' — at realizing her friend sent her a pyramid scheme kit instead of genuine support
jokes.explanation: Three words that are perfectly Moira. The shock value is earned because the setup genuinely builds warmth (Moira was touched by the gesture), making the reversal more satisfying. It's also highly quotable and deeply character-specific — this is exactly how Moira would react. The economy is exceptional; no other character on the show would deploy this phrase in this moment.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: But you could win a car.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What color is it?

jokes.text: It's a pyramid scheme for desperate housewives and struggling actors!
jokes.setup: David asks what Allez Vous is
jokes.punchline: Moira condemns it as a scheme for 'desperate housewives and struggling actors' — two categories she herself now occupies
jokes.explanation: The irony is that Moira is both a desperate housewife and a struggling actor, and she either doesn't realize it or won't acknowledge it. The show trusts the audience to make the connection without underlining it, which is confident comedy writing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, that's ridiculous. People don't just win cars from schemes like that.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Although, what exactly is the scheme? How much is the initial investment?

jokes.text: As if you could ever sell enough of this crap to get a car.
jokes.setup: David reads that you can win a Champagne Audi
jokes.punchline: Moira dismisses it — but the episode will show her trying to do exactly that
jokes.explanation: Functional dramatic irony — on rewatch, after seeing Moira host the luncheon to sell Allez Vous, this lands harder. In the moment it's a mild laugh, propped up by the irony of what's to come.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Ray won't lend me his car because he has to drive his elderly mother.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I mean, what am I supposed to do? Take the bus like some sort of... person with limited resources?

jokes.text: Well, Kristi Stubbs and her team out of Montreal have sold enough to get the car, so... don't believe everything you read, dear, please.
jokes.setup: David reads that real people have won the Audi
jokes.punchline: Moira delivers an absurd non-sequitur dismissal — 'don't believe everything you read' — applied to factual promotional material about something she just read aloud
jokes.explanation: Moira's dismissive 'don't believe everything you read' applied to the very promotional materials she just used to form her opinions is a sharp piece of character hypocrisy comedy. The pause before her delivery (indicated by 'so...') adds timing.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Where does a 92-year-old need to go?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: To the grave, John.

jokes.text: Burn it! / Except this mascara.
jokes.setup: Moira dramatically declares 'Burn it!' about the Allez Vous products
jokes.punchline: David immediately carves out an exception for the mascara, deflating her theatrics with pure pragmatism
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured two-beat joke. The 'Burn it!' is a peak Moira dramatic statement, and David's instantaneous exception cuts through it perfectly. The economy is near-perfect — three words do a lot of character work. Both characters are revealed in four total words.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That was very masculine of you, taking control like that.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I wouldn't say begging for a truck to beg for money is particularly masculine.

jokes.text: Where does a 92-year-old woman need to go?!
jokes.setup: Johnny explains that Ray can't lend him his car because he has to drive his 92-year-old mother somewhere
jokes.punchline: Johnny's entitled outrage that an elderly woman might have somewhere to be is a perfect fish-out-of-water character beat
jokes.explanation: Johnny's obliviousness is perfectly rendered. The answer to the question is obvious (the hospital, as Moira points out), which makes Johnny's indignation funnier. It's a clean character comedy beat that shows his self-centered worldview without being mean-spirited.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, do you have a moment?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: What's that?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I said, do you have a moment to talk?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Can't hear you over the mower!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's about the property line!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The property's fine!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, the property LINE!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'll do another pass!

jokes.text: Well, I think you're brave... I think there is something very masculine about the way you're taking control of this situation. / Sweetheart, I'm about to beg for a truck, in order to beg for money, I think you've gotta raise that masculinity bar just... just a little.
jokes.setup: Moira tries to frame Johnny's desperate situation in the most flattering light possible
jokes.punchline: Johnny's self-aware undercut — 'begging for a truck in order to beg for money' — is a precise, honest deflation of Moira's spin
jokes.explanation: A strong two-hander. Moira's spin is perfectly in character, and Johnny's rebuttal is economical and self-aware. 'Beg for a truck in order to beg for money' is a precise articulation of his humiliation that gets a real laugh. The 'raise the bar just a little' is a nice understated button.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You dumb son of a...

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: What was that, Johnny?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Nothing, Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, no, I heard you. You dumb son of a... what?

jokes.text: Roland mowing the lawn so loudly that Johnny can't ask to borrow the truck — Johnny's escalating attempts to be heard
jokes.setup: Johnny needs to ask Roland for a favour, but Roland is on a loud lawnmower
jokes.punchline: The lawnmower forces Johnny into a helpless, escalating shouting match that builds to him nearly insulting Roland
jokes.explanation: A functional physical comedy setup. The lawnmower gag is a classic sitcom device — not particularly original, but executed with good escalation and sets up the punchline of Johnny almost swearing at Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You can borrow my truck, but I need it back by Friday.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And it's gotta be filled up with gas. Premium, not regular.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The tires need to be checked. All four of 'em.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And I want the interior vacuumed. The seats wiped down.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The cup holders cleaned out. And the air freshener replaced with the pine scent, not the vanilla.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The windows washed inside and out. And the mirrors polished.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And if there's even a spec of dust on the dashboard, we're gonna have words.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Or I could just... take my own car.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Your loss.

jokes.text: Well, what can I do for you, you 'dumb son of a...'?
jokes.setup: Johnny was in the middle of shouting 'Turn the motor off, you dumb son of a...' when Roland finally does
jokes.punchline: Roland cheerfully repeats Johnny's partial insult back to him as a friendly greeting, revealing he heard every word
jokes.explanation: A clean, well-structured callback with a great twist: Roland isn't offended, he finds it charming. The warmth of Roland's character is established in one line. The shared laugh that follows reinforces the odd-couple friendship. Structurally very tight.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I don't use wiper fluid.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh sure, and I don't use it either. That's why my windshield's so clean.

jokes.text: The extended truck-borrowing negotiation — Roland adding conditions after already saying yes
jokes.setup: Roland says 'of course' Johnny can borrow his truck, then immediately starts adding conditions
jokes.punchline: Each condition builds (seat position, backing in, wiper fluid, radio setting) until Johnny snaps and declares he'll walk
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed escalating cringe sequence. Each new condition is more petty and unreasonable than the last, and Roland's complete obliviousness to how this lands makes it funnier. The structure is the joke — the audience knows another condition is coming, and the show delivers. Johnny's breaking point ('I'm gonna walk!') is perfectly timed.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, she clearly didn't make that money on her GED.

jokes.text: 'Yeah well, I guess your imaginary friend uses wiper fluid, right?'
jokes.setup: Johnny denies ever touching the wiper fluid in Roland's truck
jokes.punchline: Roland's passive-aggressive implication that if not Johnny, then his 'imaginary friend' must be responsible
jokes.explanation: A standout line within the escalation sequence. The 'imaginary friend' framing is original and specific — it's the kind of passive-aggressive logic that feels true to the character. Punches above the average line in this scene.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, her face is certainly... different. I'm assuming that's a choice she made, and not a reaction to some sort of allergic situation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The cheekbones are very... pronounced. It's like watching someone who's been caught in a wind tunnel.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And the lips! They're so plump, I'm worried she's going to tip over if she leans forward.

jokes.text: 'The radio was at a four. I like it at a ten, so...' — Roland's final condition before Johnny walks
jokes.setup: Johnny is already at his limit after seats, reversing, wiper fluid, and tire air
jokes.punchline: Roland adds the most trivial condition yet — radio volume — delivered with total earnestness
jokes.explanation: The perfect final straw. After asking for serious things (tire pressure, wiper fluid), asking someone to change your radio preset is so petty it becomes absurd. This is the line that breaks Johnny, and rightly so. Great escalation structure.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So what are the tips and tricks?

dialogue.speaker: MLM Speaker (Video)
dialogue.line: Well, the key to success is to think like a successful person.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Right, but what does that mean?

dialogue.speaker: MLM Speaker (Video)
dialogue.line: It means you need to believe in yourself and your product.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, but specifically—

dialogue.speaker: MLM Speaker (Video)
dialogue.line: And once you believe, others will believe too.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, but how do I actually make money?

dialogue.speaker: MLM Speaker (Video)
dialogue.line: By helping others succeed, you succeed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not getting anything from this.

jokes.text: I don't touch the wiper fluid! / Well, okay, somebody did. / Yeah well, I guess your 'imaginary friend' uses wiper fluid, right?
jokes.setup: Roland mentions the wiper fluid was used more than usual
jokes.punchline: Johnny denies touching it; Roland's passive-aggressive 'imaginary friend' comment is a tiny gem of petty escalation
jokes.explanation: A nice mid-escalation beat within the truck negotiation sequence. Roland's 'imaginary friend' is a weird, specific insult that fits his character. Not a standout joke on its own but contributes well to the running escalation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, why is the video talking in circles?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's a scam, David.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We should do it.

jokes.text: The radio was at a four. I like it at a ten, so...
jokes.setup: Johnny has already agreed to multiple petty conditions
jokes.punchline: Roland's final absurd demand — the radio volume — is the straw that breaks Johnny's back and makes him walk
jokes.explanation: The perfect final escalation beat. The radio being 'at a four' is so mundane and so petty that it functions as the ideal breaking point for Johnny. The payoff — Johnny walking — is earned by the careful escalation of the sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We're going to sell the products at a luncheon.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A luncheon?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, it's more sophisticated than a lunch.

jokes.text: No, it isn't! She didn't finish high school.
jokes.setup: The Allez Vous video claims you can make $600,000 a year — that's $50,000 a month, $7,000 a week
jokes.punchline: Moira, watching the video, instantly fact-checks the math with 'No, it isn't!' and attributes the error to the presenter not finishing high school
jokes.explanation: A sharp, economical joke. Moira is right — $7,000/week is actually ~$364,000/year, not $600,000. So her interruption is simultaneously snobbish AND mathematically correct. The 'she didn't finish high school' explanation is perfectly Moira. One of the episode's better jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I'm being difficult. I'm on this new cleanse and I'm just really irritable.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's basically just hot water and lemon, but apparently it makes you a nightmare.

jokes.text: Look at the work she's had done, it's grotesque.
jokes.setup: Moira and David are watching the Allez Vous promotional video
jokes.punchline: Rather than engaging with the content, Moira's only commentary is a withering critique of Justine's cosmetic surgery
jokes.explanation: Character comedy through cattiness. Moira's complete inability to engage with anything on its merits — she can only critique appearance — is funny, though the joke itself is fairly predictable. The irony that Moira herself has presumably 'had work done' adds a layer.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It's not like there's broken glass in it.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh thank God. I thought you were going to say there was.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, no. I was just saying that to reassure you.

jokes.text: Okay, where are the tips and tricks? We've been watching this for five minutes, she's talking in circles!
jokes.setup: The Allez Vous video keeps promising 'tips and tricks' without delivering them
jokes.punchline: David's frustrated observation that the video is circular is both an accurate critique of MLM marketing and a funny character beat from someone who was supposed to be above this
jokes.explanation: Good satirical beat about MLM sales tactics. The fact that David is now invested enough to complain about the video's structure reveals how far he's already been sucked in. Solid observational comedy with character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, I'm just trying to help. You've been incredibly rude to everyone today.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, that is so rude of you to say.

jokes.text: But if your father's willing to go out there and sell his soul, dear, the least we can do is try to sell this garbage.
jokes.setup: Moira dismissed Allez Vous as a scam
jokes.punchline: She now rationalizes trying to sell it by referencing Johnny's humiliation, and refers to her own product as 'garbage'
jokes.explanation: The self-aware labeling of their own product as 'garbage' while committing to selling it is funny. Moira's logic here — Johnny's suffering justifies their participation in a scam — is perfectly Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, what do you think? Pretty great, right?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh, it's absolutely stunning. Really, truly one of the finest automobiles I've ever seen.

jokes.text: You sell you. You sell you using Allez Vous.
jokes.setup: Moira has decided they should try to sell the Allez Vous products
jokes.punchline: She delivers this as profound wisdom — a corporate MLM mantra — with complete sincerity and theatrical gravitas
jokes.explanation: The rhythm and delivery of 'You sell you using Allez Vous' is inherently funny — it sounds like an MLM slogan written by someone who just learned what alliteration is. Moira delivering it as though it's the secret of life is perfect character comedy. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Bob, I appreciate your enthusiasm, but I need you to understand something. A test drive is a very intimate experience. It's a moment between a man and a machine. A little peccadillo, if you will.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: A peccadillo is a minor fault or sin, Johnny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, the point is, I need to be alone with the car. It's romantic.

jokes.text: Okay, we're going to have a luncheon. A luncheon? Mm-hmm, a luncheon for our little townie friends.
jokes.setup: Moira announces her sales strategy
jokes.punchline: The condescension packed into 'our little townie friends' as she describes people she wants to exploit for money is a perfect class-comedy beat
jokes.explanation: The diminutive 'little' in 'little townie friends' does a lot of work. Moira's plan to exploit the community she claims to want to celebrate is pure Moira Rose. The hushed, conspiratorial tone David's 'A luncheon?' implies makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: ♪ Going down to the unemployment office, gonna get myself some checks... ♪

jokes.text: System, support, tips, tricks, community, opportunity, tips...
jokes.setup: Moira is watching the Allez Vous promotional video off-screen while the scene cuts to the diner
jokes.punchline: Her murmured mantra-like repetition of MLM buzzwords trails off into 'tips' again, circling back and revealing the video still hasn't delivered them
jokes.explanation: A clever audio joke that confirms David's earlier observation — the video really IS circular. Moira's zombie-like recitation of the buzzwords while a scene change happens is a nice formal trick. The trailing 'tips...' proves nothing has been resolved.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, Ted is too nice. It's exhausting. He's always so... helpful and considerate.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? Ted's great.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, but like, in high school I was really mean to him. I called him names, I excluded him from things...

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You bullied Ted?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but now he's so nice to me it just makes me feel guilty all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Wow.

jokes.text: First day of a cleanse, so she's a little whoo!
jokes.setup: Ted received the wrong food order and Alexis is trying to manage the situation awkwardly
jokes.punchline: Alexis explains Twyla's lack of apology by casually mentioning she's on a cleanse, as though that fully explains erratic behavior
jokes.explanation: A quick throwaway joke. The 'cleanse' explanation as a catch-all excuse for weird behavior is recognizable and mildly funny. Not particularly innovative but lands as a light beat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I bullied Ted because he was polite. Like, aggressively polite. He would hold doors open and say 'excuse me' and it was just... I couldn't stand it.

jokes.text: It's not like there's broken glass in it. / There's broken glass?
jokes.setup: Ted is trying to reassure everyone that the wrong food order is fine
jokes.punchline: Twyla, apparently on a cleanse and not at her best, genuinely hears 'broken glass' as a real concern — revealing she wasn't following the conversation
jokes.explanation: A clean, tight two-beat joke. The 'not like there's broken glass in it' is meant to be obviously rhetorical, but Twyla's cleanse-addled brain takes it literally. The panic in 'There's broken glass?' is a perfect character beat. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Come on, come on, come on...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, no, no, NO!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Are you KIDDING me right now?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: This is UNBELIEVABLE!

jokes.text: I do not like what this cleanse is doing to your brain. You're being very rude! / I'm sorry for yelling.
jokes.setup: Alexis accuses Ted of being rude due to his cleanse-altered state, then immediately yells at him about it
jokes.punchline: She immediately apologizes for yelling — revealing that she, not Ted, was the rude one all along, and the cleanse is affecting HER brain
jokes.explanation: The irony of Alexis accusing Ted of being rude while being rude herself, then immediately apologizing, is a compact and effective character comedy moment. The self-correction comes too fast to be genuine growth, which makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, you're being all mysterious about where you're going? That's fine. I don't need to know. I'm just the guy who owns the motel where you're staying. But sure, keep your little secrets. I respect that.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'd love to take this baby out for a test drive. / Yeah? I've got the keys, let's take 'er for a spin. / Not with you, Bob.
jokes.setup: Bob offers to come along for the test drive
jokes.punchline: Johnny immediately and bluntly refuses — 'Not with you, Bob' — revealing his intention to use the test drive to get to Elmdale
jokes.explanation: The bluntness of 'Not with you, Bob' is perfect. Johnny was being so performatively friendly a moment ago, making the sudden candor funnier. The euphemistic 'peccadillo' he uses to justify wanting to drive alone is an additional layer of character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So where are we going, Johnny? You've been so secretive about it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're going to the hardware store, Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The hardware store? Why would you not just tell me that in the first place? I didn't even want to know!

jokes.text: You know what I mean? / No, I don't.
jokes.setup: Johnny has just delivered an elaborate, philosophical explanation for why he wants to test drive alone ('just me and the machine')
jokes.punchline: Bob simply: 'No, I don't.' — refusing to validate the nonsense
jokes.explanation: Bob's flat refusal to engage with Johnny's justification is a clean, economical punchline. It's the straight-man reaction that deflates Johnny's elaborate pretense perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So where are we going, Johnny?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I've told you three times already.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I know, I know, I'm just making conversation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're going to the doctor.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. That's personal, Johnny. I didn't need to know that.

jokes.text: Okay, but no lefts, okay? Because they lead to the highway, and I really don't want her on the highway.
jokes.setup: Johnny has gotten Bob to agree to a test drive and describes vaguely going 'up the road a bit'
jokes.punchline: Bob's 'no lefts' rule — because they lead to the highway — is both reasonable and, in context, a direct foreshadowing of exactly what Johnny will do
jokes.explanation: The 'no lefts' rule functions as setup for the later highway payoff. In context it's a good character beat for Bob — the specificity of 'no lefts' is funny. Gains a rewatch bonus because it pays off so cleanly later.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And that's why Herb-scription cosmetics are not just a beauty product, they're a lifestyle choice!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, why are there seventeen boxes of face serum under the sink?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, I've explained this. I'm a regional sales associate now.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You bought seventeen boxes yesterday.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you can never have too much inventory.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And the forty jars of anti-wrinkle cream in your closet?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Those are samples for potential clients.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: There are literally products in the toilet tank.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, David, why are you snooping in the toilet tank?

jokes.text: Why would I take it on the highway? ♪ driving down that highway of life ♪
jokes.setup: Johnny has just reassured Bob he won't take the car on the highway
jokes.punchline: Immediate cut to Johnny singing about 'that highway of life' while clearly driving on the highway
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best structural joke. The instant ironic cut from Johnny's 'Why would I take it on the highway?' to him singing on the highway is perfectly timed. The improvised song ('driving down that highway of life... going to pick me up some money') is both funny and reveals exactly what he's doing. The structure here is exceptional — one of the cleaner comedic reversals in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Ted, you have to leave the party.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh, well, I'm actually having a really nice time.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yes, but you need to go. Now.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh, but your friends have been so welcoming, and the appetizers are really—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted! Can you just, for once in your life, be rude? Be mean? Say something horrible!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I... I don't think I can do that.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: This is exactly what I'm talking about!

jokes.text: Well, I was a different person then, a lot changed since after high school. / So what did he do to make you such a teenage cliché?
jokes.setup: Alexis admits she used to pin Ted against a locker
jokes.punchline: Stevie's 'teenage cliché' response is a sharp, funny framing of Alexis's bully backstory
jokes.explanation: Stevie's dry wit cuts through Alexis's self-justification efficiently. 'Teenage cliché' is a pointed and slightly unexpected framing that gets a good chuckle.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He's being so decisive-y right now.

jokes.text: He always said hi when he passed me in the halls, and for some reason, it bugged me. / Yeah, I can see that.
jokes.setup: Stevie asks what Ted did to provoke Alexis bullying him
jokes.punchline: Ted's crime was saying hi in the halls. Stevie's 'Yeah, I can see that' — deadpan, directed at Alexis — implies that Alexis is the kind of person who would find that threatening
jokes.explanation: Stevie's 'Yeah, I can see that' is a brilliantly ambiguous line — ostensibly sympathetic but actually a perfect deadpan indictment of Alexis's character. It's subtle and smart. The revelation that Ted's crime was being friendly is itself a funny absurdist touch.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, darling, I've discovered the most revolutionary anti-aging serum. You simply must try it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, I'm in my twenties.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I know, sweetie. That's why you need to start now. Prevention is key.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Are you saying I look old?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, no. I'm saying you look like you're about to.

jokes.text: Stevie's revelation that she used to pin Ted against a locker in high school
jokes.setup: Stevie is complaining about Ted being 'too nice' and 'too generous'
jokes.punchline: She casually reveals she physically bullied him in high school, which completely recontextualizes her current discomfort
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection — the complaint about Ted's niceness becomes a confession of past bullying. The reason she bullied him ('he always said hi') is a perfectly absurd inversion of normal bullying logic.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: You people are delusional if you think anyone's gonna buy this crap. I've seen better sales pitches from a concussed squirrel.

jokes.text: So he's bugging you. / What? No! Oh my God, no! / Oh yes.
jokes.setup: Alexis has been going on about Ted — his generosity, his niceness, how she wants to grab him by the sweater
jokes.punchline: Stevie's casual 'So he's bugging you' — as a coded reading of obvious attraction — and her 'Oh yes' in the face of Alexis's denial is a perfect knowing exchange
jokes.explanation: Stevie's quiet romantic insight is funny partly because it's so obvious and partly because her 'Oh yes' after Alexis's denial is delivered so calmly. It's a nice relationship-comedy beat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I need to speak to someone about expediting my application. I have a very tight schedule.

dialogue.speaker: Clerk
dialogue.line: Sir, these things take time.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I don't have time. I have a golf game on Thursday.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, I can see that.' — Alexis's reaction to Stevie confessing she bullied someone for saying hi
jokes.setup: Stevie explains she bullied Ted because he said hi in the halls
jokes.punchline: Alexis responds with total casual acceptance: 'Yeah, I can see that' — as if this is completely reasonable
jokes.explanation: Three words doing heavy lifting. Alexis's casual validation of absurd bullying behavior tells us everything about her own background — she comes from a world where this makes sense. Perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Clerk
dialogue.line: Have you ever been convicted of a felony?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, not convicted.

dialogue.speaker: Clerk
dialogue.line: Do you have any experience in this field?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I have money. Isn't that experience?

dialogue.speaker: Clerk
dialogue.line: Can you start immediately?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm going to need at least three weeks to have my assistant set up my office. And I don't have an assistant yet.

jokes.text: I never return food, I think that's so rude.
jokes.setup: Alexis has just spent several minutes trying to get Ted's food order corrected and making everything more awkward
jokes.punchline: She now declares that returning food is rude — the exact thing she's been trying to do — completely unaware of the contradiction
jokes.explanation: A neat ironic callback. Alexis's self-awareness deficit is a consistent source of comedy — she cannot see that her stated values and her behavior are completely opposed. The timing here, after all the breakfast chaos, makes it land well.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm an employer. I have employees. Surely that qualifies me for employee benefits.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, you can't get employee benefits for being an employer.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, that seems like a significant oversight in the system.

jokes.text: Johnny singing happily along with the radio — immediately followed by the car breaking down with clattering/hissing sounds
jokes.setup: Johnny is cheerfully singing and scatting to the radio on his illicit highway drive
jokes.punchline: The car immediately breaks down — engine clattering, hissing, Johnny saying 'Oh no! Son of a bum! Son of a bitch!'
jokes.explanation: Classic comedic timing — the immediate juxtaposition of joy and disaster. Johnny's escalating expletives ('son of a bum... son of a bitch') reveal his attempt at self-censorship collapsing under stress. The physical situation (steam, clattering) is vivid even in transcript form.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, you're doing a wonderful job.

jokes.text: Oh, just the general downtown area will be fine.
jokes.setup: Roland has come to help Johnny after the car broke down and is now driving him to Elmdale; he asks where in Elmdale
jokes.punchline: Johnny's deliberately vague 'general downtown area' reveals he doesn't want to admit where he's going
jokes.explanation: A nice comedic beat of Johnny's shame. The studied vagueness of 'general downtown area' is funny and sets up Roland's escalating interrogation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think it went rather well, actually. Did you see how engaged everyone was? Particularly when they were leaving.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And the energy in the room was electric. Granted, most of it was static electricity from people frantically gathering their belongings, but energy nonetheless.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm already planning the next event. I'm thinking we go bigger. More aggressive. Perhaps we hold them hostage until they commit to a starter kit.

jokes.text: Okay, don't wanna tell me where we're going, interesting.
jokes.setup: Johnny gives a deliberately vague answer about their destination
jokes.punchline: Roland's 'interesting' is a perfectly relaxed, low-key passive-aggressive response that signals he's going to keep pressing
jokes.explanation: Roland's one-word 'interesting' is funnier than most longer responses could be. It's the exact right amount of comment — enough to signal he knows something's up, not enough to resolve the tension.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Wait, you're all already in an MLM?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh yes, I'm a Regional Vice President.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I'm a District Manager.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I'm a National Executive Director of Operations.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I'm actually a Continental Ambassador.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Global Empress.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm the... Supreme Overlord of All Pyramids.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: That's not real.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Yeah, Moira, that's not a thing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Johnny's escalating evasion about where he's going — 'a financial guy... a financial matter... a guy I'm doing business with' — as Roland probes
jokes.setup: Roland keeps pressing Johnny on their destination; Johnny keeps giving progressively vaguer answers
jokes.punchline: Johnny finally breaks: 'THE UNEMPLOYMENT OFFICE! Okay?! I'm applying for unemployment.'
jokes.explanation: A well-structured escalation leading to a cathartic confession. Each of Johnny's progressively sillier euphemisms ('financial guy,' 'financial matter,' 'guy I'm doing business with') gets funnier, and Roland's dogged pressing is perfectly timed. The release when Johnny finally shouts his secret is very satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: In a town this size, the multi-level marketing structure becomes rather... recursive.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I was selling to Roland, who was selling to David, who was selling to you, who was selling back to me.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: So we were all just... buying from each other?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: In circles. We created a pyramid scheme where the pyramid was actually a circle, and we were all at the bottom of it.

jokes.text: The unemployment office! Okay? I'm applying for unemployment.
jokes.setup: Roland has been interrogating Johnny for several minutes about where they're going; Johnny has cycled through 'financial guy,' 'business meeting,' and 'a guy'
jokes.punchline: Johnny finally breaks and blurts out 'The unemployment office!' — the confession is the punchline, earned by the escalating interrogation
jokes.explanation: The payoff to a well-constructed escalation. Johnny's humiliated confession is both funny and humanizing. The structure — cycling through evasive synonyms before cracking — is classic sitcom but executed with real character specificity here, because Johnny's shame is so palpable.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Marnie! Oh my God, it's been what, thirty years? You look exactly the same. Well, not exactly. Your face has changed. And your body. But in a good way, I think. Anyway, I've thought about you a lot over the years. Mostly in the shower. Sometimes in the car. One time during a city council meeting, but I was very discreet about it.

dialogue.speaker: Marnie
dialogue.line: Oh. Um. That's... nice?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, I still have the motel receipt from that night. I keep it in my wallet. Next to my driver's license and pictures of my kids, but closer to the front.

dialogue.speaker: Marnie
dialogue.line: I should probably get going.

jokes.text: Jeez pal, your business is your business. I don't know why you're telling me!
jokes.setup: Johnny has just confessed to going to the unemployment office after being grilled by Roland
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately affects complete disinterest — 'I don't know why you're telling me!' — despite having just interrogated Johnny mercilessly for it
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The reversal is perfect and completely unexpected: Roland spent two full minutes extracting this information through persistent interrogation, then immediately acts as though Johnny volunteered it out of nowhere. The character specificity is excellent — Roland isn't being cruel, he's just oblivious to his own behavior. This is the kind of joke that makes you rewind to appreciate. Economy is exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I don't qualify for unemployment. I need to find work immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... didn't you work for me at the store?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, Roland, I never worked for you.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh right, right, right... I'm remembering it now. You worked for me for... two years.

jokes.text: David is setting out some hors d'oeuvres in our adjoining suite — and the suite is covered in Allez Vous products
jokes.setup: Moira welcomes guests and promises David has set up hors d'oeuvres in the adjoining suite
jokes.punchline: The suite is conspicuously covered in Allez Vous products — the 'hors d'oeuvres' are a Trojan horse for a sales pitch
jokes.explanation: The luncheon-as-sales-trap is a solid sitcom premise. The visual of the suite covered in MLM products when guests expected food is a good visual joke, even inferred from dialogue.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So you need to list your job duties for the unemployment office.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, that's right.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, let's see... You were in charge of... moisturizing the storefront windows with your own saliva.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What? No, I wasn't!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And twice a week you had to polish the merchandise with your hair.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I'm not putting that down!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, and your primary responsibility was standing in the window in your underwear to attract customers.

jokes.text: I just didn't want to put such high quality paraben and paba free product on the floor!
jokes.setup: David is pretending his failure to 'clean up' the Allez Vous products was accidental
jokes.punchline: His justification — genuinely concerned about putting quality product on the floor — accidentally reveals he's already bought into the MLM's marketing language
jokes.explanation: The 'paraben and paba free' language reveals that David has absorbed the MLM product descriptions despite his cynicism. It's a small but effective character detail showing how insidiously these schemes work.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I had to let Johnny go. He was cutting the gravy with water.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I said, 'Johnny, you can't cut the gravy with water.' He said, 'Why not?' I said, 'Because then it's not gravy, it's just hot salt water with the dreams of gravy in it.'

jokes.text: Can you not just say no for one time, Ted?!
jokes.setup: Ted was invited by Moira and has agreed to stay for the Allez Vous luncheon; Alexis wants to leave
jokes.punchline: Alexis's exasperated demand that Ted 'just say no for once' is rich coming from someone who earlier lectured him about returning food being rude
jokes.explanation: Alexis's frustration with Ted's agreeableness is funny given her own contradictory nature. The line works as character comedy — Alexis is attracted to and repelled by the same quality.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted, I'm so sorry. I got you a gift as an apology.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh, Alexis, that's very thoughtful of you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know you're going to love it. It's this amazing skincare line that I've been meaning to get rid of—I mean, share with you.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Is this... an MLM product?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, it was sitting in my closet and nobody wanted it, so...

jokes.text: Jocelyn, you can hold this lip plumper. / Ronnie, this is a mineral moisturizer, you could keep it on your lap, or sample it.
jokes.setup: Moira is distributing Allez Vous products to her guests at the 'luncheon'
jokes.punchline: The casual authority with which Moira assigns products to specific guests — as though she's seating them at a state dinner — is perfectly absurd
jokes.explanation: Moira hosting an MLM sales pitch with the gravitas of a diplomatic reception is inherently funny. The specific assignments ('you could keep it on your lap, or sample it' — options!) reveal her complete unawareness of how the situation looks.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: My family sees kindness as a weakness. Like, my dad literally told me that showing emotion is for people who can't afford therapy.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: That's... that's not healthy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know! But try telling that to a man who considers eye contact a personal attack.

jokes.text: Alexis, you'd like this product. It's all about aging, and dry skin.
jokes.setup: David is being pressed into participating in the Allez Vous pitch
jokes.punchline: He takes a swipe at Alexis by suggesting the anti-aging product is for her — implying she looks old and has dry skin
jokes.explanation: Classic sibling comedy — David using the sales pitch as cover for a personal insult. The beat is quick and effective. Alexis's 'Oh my God, ew, David!' is the perfect reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Can you just be mean to me? Like, as a roleplay thing?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I don't think I need to roleplay that with you.

jokes.text: David, I know you're excited to be part of the fastest growing multilevel marketing community on the continent, but honestly! / There's something wrong with your face.
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to rein David in from his sibling taunting
jokes.punchline: David's response to being told to stop is to tell Moira 'there's something wrong with your face' — a petty, childish non-sequitur that is perfectly David
jokes.explanation: The setup is Moira's elaborate corporate-speak reprimand ('fastest growing multilevel marketing community on the continent'), and the punchline is David's utterly childish 'there's something wrong with your face.' The contrast between her theatrical formality and his kindergarten-level insult is the joke. Highly quotable and deeply character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Just try to be mean to me.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Okay, fine. You're shallow, you're self-absorbed, and you've never had a real relationship that lasted more than a few months because you get bored the second someone actually cares about you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wow, okay, that's... that's actually really mean.

jokes.text: With my face? / Well, if any of you do happen to have questions about the products, or how to make up to 600,000 dollars a year, I suppose I'd be willing to share with you what I know.
jokes.setup: David has just told Moira there's something wrong with her face
jokes.punchline: Moira's brief moment of self-consciousness ('With my face?') is quickly suppressed as she pivots back to her sales pitch with a beautifully fake modesty ('I suppose I'd be willing to share')
jokes.explanation: Moira's lightning recovery from David's insult — pivoting seamlessly from wounded vanity to false modesty about her expertise — is great character comedy. The 'I suppose I'd be willing' is perfectly calibrated false humility.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm gonna make you an offer you can't refuse.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Is that The Godfather?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The what?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: The Godfather. The movie.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, no. This is just how I talk now.

jokes.text: Please, eat, drink, to your possible beauty!
jokes.setup: Moira concludes her sales pitch moment with a toast
jokes.punchline: 'To your possible beauty!' is a perfectly backhanded toast — implying their beauty is not yet achieved but could theoretically be, with Allez Vous products
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's best lines. 'To your possible beauty' is a stunning piece of character writing — it's simultaneously a sales pitch, an insult, and a toast, delivered by someone who thinks it's a compliment. The word 'possible' does enormous comedic and character work. Highly quotable, very original, and completely Moira. Economy is exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm gonna make him an offer he can't refuse.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's The Godfather.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, no, no. It's not a movie. It's something I came up with.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, that's literally The Godfather.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I don't know what that is.

jokes.text: Yes, Johnny Rose, I have an appointment to get some money. / Get some money? / I need a cheque, preferably today. Ideally in the next few minutes.
jokes.setup: Johnny calls the unemployment office to announce his arrival
jokes.punchline: His description of the appointment as being 'to get some money' and request for a cheque 'preferably today, ideally in the next few minutes' reveals he has no idea how unemployment insurance works
jokes.explanation: Johnny treating the unemployment office like a bank or a restaurant is perfectly in character. The escalating specificity ('preferably today... ideally in the next few minutes... 'cause I have somebody waiting in the car') is a great comedic build. His obliviousness to bureaucracy is both funny and poignant.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So I need you to do me a favor. I need you to pick up some Kleenex for me.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Of course.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And I need you to grab me a caddy for it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Sure.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm serious.

jokes.text: Well, we don't just give out money.
jokes.setup: Johnny has just casually asked for a cheque 'preferably today'
jokes.punchline: Marnie's flat 'Well, we don't just give out money' is the perfect bureaucratic undercut to Johnny's entitlement
jokes.explanation: A clean, economical straight-man line. The delivery implied by 'we don't just give out money' perfectly represents the bureaucratic wall Johnny has no idea how to navigate.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I told you that car wasn't going to make it on the highway.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, it made it further than I expected.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: It broke down two miles from here.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Exactly. Two miles further than I expected.

jokes.text: 'I need a cheque, preferably today. Ideally in the next few minutes, 'cause I have somebody waiting in the car.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to apply for unemployment insurance
jokes.punchline: He treats the entire process like ordering at a counter, adding the time constraint of 'someone in the car' as if the officer should expedite service
jokes.explanation: The 'somebody waiting in the car' detail is a brilliant character-specific touch — Johnny still thinks he operates in a world where his personal convenience can expedite bureaucracy. His imperiousness in this moment is both funny and poignant.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: This car is a complete disaster. It's falling apart, Bob.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I'm aware of the condition, but I'm willing to offer you fifteen thousand dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Fifteen thousand? Bob, you couldn't give this away. It's worthless.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, I think you're being unfair. I'm going to raise my offer to seventeen thousand.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You're insane. The transmission doesn't even work!

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Eighteen thousand. Final offer.

jokes.text: Your name? / Johnny Rose. / Last employer? / Johnny Rose. / And how long did you work for yourself? / All my life.
jokes.setup: The intake interview begins
jokes.punchline: Johnny's 'last employer' is himself, and 'how long did you work for yourself? — All my life' is a quietly resonant answer that's both funny and a little sad
jokes.explanation: A genuinely elegant joke. The setup is the mechanical form-filling (name, employer), and the punchline ('All my life') lands on two levels — funny because it's a literal answer to a bureaucratic question, and poignant because it speaks to Johnny's identity. This is Schitt's Creek at its best: comedy that carries emotional weight. The structure is impeccable.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait! Johnny, come back! I'll throw in these premium floor mats absolutely free!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Bob, the car doesn't have an engine.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Yes, but look at how clean the floors will be.

jokes.text: And your reason for being out of work? / I sold my business, and I got screwed by my business manager.
jokes.setup: Standard unemployment intake form question
jokes.punchline: Johnny's blunt 'I got screwed by my business manager' in response to a form question is both funny and the raw truth of the family's situation
jokes.explanation: The casual bluntness of 'I got screwed by my business manager' delivered as though filling in a form is a nice comedic beat. It's also the most honest Johnny has been about his situation all episode.

jokes.text: I don't think you're understanding, I employed people, hundreds of people. / You see, and now I'm unemployed. / But that doesn't mean you qualify for unemployment. / I lost my job! / You lost your business.
jokes.setup: Marnie explains that Johnny doesn't qualify for unemployment because he was self-employed
jokes.punchline: Johnny's inability to grasp the distinction between 'losing a job' and 'losing a business' — and Marnie's precise corrections — is a comedic clash of worldviews
jokes.explanation: The volley of 'I lost my job!' / 'You lost your business' is a sharp, quick exchange that perfectly encapsulates the class comedy at the heart of the episode. Marnie's bureaucratic precision cuts through Johnny's self-pity each time.

jokes.text: Johnny's speech about employing hundreds of people and therefore deserving money — 'am I the only one who understands how an economy works?!'
jokes.setup: The officer tells Johnny he doesn't qualify because he didn't contribute to the unemployment program
jokes.punchline: Johnny's response escalates from explanation to demands to lecturing the officer on macroeconomics — completely failing to understand he's the one who's wrong
jokes.explanation: 'Am I the only one who understands how an economy works around here?!' is a great line — a rich man who created his own problems lecturing a government employee on economics. The escalation from personal plea to grand economic theory is very Johnny, and the line is quotable.

jokes.text: Okay, let me walk you through this. You see, I need money to get back on my feet so I can employ more people, that might actually end up saving you money in the long run — now, am I the only one who understands how an economy works around here?!
jokes.setup: Marnie has denied Johnny's claim; Johnny tries a different angle
jokes.punchline: Johnny's elaborate economic theory — essentially 'give me money so I can eventually employ people so you'll save money' — delivered as obvious wisdom that Marnie is too dull to understand, is perfectly absurd
jokes.explanation: 'Am I the only one who understands how an economy works?' is a brilliant line because Johnny is both completely wrong in his application of economic logic AND convinced he's the smartest person in the room. The character integration is excellent — only Johnny Rose would respond to bureaucratic rejection by delivering an economic lecture.

jokes.text: I'd like to talk to your supervisor, please. / I am the supervisor. / And, may I say, doing a fantastic job.
jokes.setup: Johnny, after being repeatedly denied, escalates by asking for the supervisor
jokes.punchline: Marnie is the supervisor; Johnny's immediate pivot to hollow flattery — 'may I say, doing a fantastic job' — is a desperate and transparent save
jokes.explanation: The structure here is excellent: classic escalation (asking for supervisor), classic sitcom reversal (she IS the supervisor), and then a perfectly timed recovery compliment that fools no one. 'May I say, doing a fantastic job' is highly quotable and reveals everything about Johnny's desperation. One of the better jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: And who's interested? / Well, it doesn't look like anybody's really interested. / Get out!
jokes.setup: Moira and David are trying to sell Allez Vous at the luncheon
jokes.punchline: After a pause (implied silence from the guests), David's 'Well, it doesn't look like anybody's really interested' is delivered flatly, and Moira's 'Get out!' is a brief explosion of frustration
jokes.explanation: The implied beat of nobody being interested followed by David's flat observation and Moira's tiny explosion is a good cringe beat. David narrating the obvious failure while Moira desperately tries to recover is a nice character dynamic.

jokes.text: Moira, honey, that Champagne Audi is really hard to get. / Take it from me, Roland and I have been Allez Vous reps for two years, and we're still driving that old truck.
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to sell Allez Vous with talk of the Champagne Audi prize
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn reveals she and Roland are already Allez Vous reps — and have been for two years with nothing to show for it — undercutting the entire sales pitch
jokes.explanation: A good structural joke — the reveal that Jocelyn and Roland are already reps who haven't won the car is both funny and completely deflating. It pays off the Champagne Audi thread from the opening while also showing that Moira's 'exclusive opportunity' is not exclusive at all.

jokes.text: Sorry, you're... you're an Allez Vous consultant? / I'm actually an executive area manager. / I'm a district manager. / I'm an executive district manager. / I gave up.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn reveals she's an Allez Vous rep; other guests turn out to also be reps
jokes.punchline: A rapid-fire escalation of fake MLM titles — executive area manager, district manager, executive district manager — capped by someone who 'gave up'
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best sequences. The escalating MLM title one-upmanship is funny on its own, but 'I gave up' as the final beat is perfect — it acknowledges the absurdity of the entire MLM hierarchy while being a devastatingly simple punchline. The economy is exceptional; four progressively pompous titles followed by two words that say everything. Well-structured escalation with a perfect button.

jokes.text: In two weeks we had sold everything to the other half of town, and vice versa. / That's... that's funny. / It became a bit incestuous, yeah, very small sales pool.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn explains the history of Allez Vous in Schitt's Creek
jokes.punchline: The town sold everything to itself — everyone sold to everyone else — and then there was no one left to sell to. The MLM self-destructs due to the town's small size
jokes.explanation: A sharp observation about how MLMs collapse in small communities — everyone sold to everyone, then ran out of people. 'Incestuous' is a funny word choice for a sales pool. Moira's stunned 'That's... that's funny' is a good reaction.

jokes.text: Somehow, we got off on the wrong foot. Okay? So if I in any way gave you the impression that— / I am locking up now, Mr. Rose.
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to salvage the unemployment interview after his economic lecture
jokes.punchline: Marnie simply announces she's locking up, completely ending the conversation mid-apology
jokes.explanation: Marnie's flat refusal to engage with Johnny's apology — just announcing she's locking up — is a great bureaucratic dismissal. It's funny because she's not even rude about it; she's just done.

jokes.text: Marnie and I were, um, how shall I say it? 'Close friends' in high school. Isn't that right, Marnie? / Okay, that's enough of that.
jokes.setup: Roland recognizes Marnie from outside the office
jokes.punchline: Roland's theatrical 'how shall I say it?' and air-quote 'close friends' reveals a past relationship, which Marnie immediately shuts down
jokes.explanation: Roland's hammy theatrics about his past with Marnie are funny, and her immediate 'Okay, that's enough of that' is a perfect comic suppression. The juxtaposition of Roland's enjoyment and Marnie's discomfort is classic cringe comedy.

jokes.text: (Chuckles) Marnie likes the bad boys. / Roland! / Are you taking care of my bad boy over there?
jokes.setup: Roland is reveling in embarrassing Marnie with their shared history
jokes.punchline: Roland calling Johnny Rose — buttoned-up former corporate executive — 'my bad boy' is a deeply absurd characterization that Marnie wants no part of
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of Johnny Rose being described as a 'bad boy' is genuinely funny. Roland's enthusiastic deployment of this characterization while Marnie visibly wants to disappear is a strong cringe beat. Character integration is good — only Roland would do this.

jokes.text: Well, unfortunately, Mr. Rose doesn't qualify for unemployment because he's never had a job. / Oh well, didn't he tell you about the job he was doing for me? / No, what job? / You didn't tell her? / No-no, not specifically, I-I-uh...
jokes.setup: Marnie explains Johnny doesn't qualify; Roland intervenes with a convenient job story
jokes.punchline: Roland and Johnny's panicked improvisation — Roland volunteering information, Johnny scrambling to catch up — is a great comedic partnership moment
jokes.explanation: The improvised cover story scene is good character comedy — Roland plunging in enthusiastically while Johnny desperately tries to follow his lead. The dynamic between them is well-established by this point.

jokes.text: What were some of the things you fetched? / You fetched like uh... / Coffee. / Coffee, you'd get that... / Sometimes you'd have to... / Get a hankie. / No, you'd pick up, you know... / Cleaning, his dry cleaning. / Yeah, you'd have to do that... / Just when he didn't have time.
jokes.setup: Marnie asks for specifics about Johnny's 'job'; Roland and Johnny must improvise a convincing job description
jokes.punchline: Their overlapping improvisation — where Johnny keeps trying to interject dignity and Roland keeps overriding him with more degrading tasks — builds to boot polishing
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's finest sustained sequences. The overlapping dialogue rhythm — Roland eagerly adding ever-more-demeaning tasks while Johnny desperately tries to soften them — is exceptional character writing. The escalation from coffee to hankie to dry cleaning to boot polishing is perfectly calibrated. Johnny's 'Just when he didn't have time' is a touching attempt to maintain dignity that makes it funnier. Character integration is near-perfect.

jokes.text: He was a hell of a worker and I really, really needed him, because you know how busy I can get. / (Sly chuckle) Yes I do. / (Chuckles)
jokes.setup: Roland is wrapping up his character reference for Johnny
jokes.punchline: Marnie's 'Yes I do' — with a sly chuckle — reveals that she and Roland's past relationship involved knowing how 'busy' Roland gets, adding an unexpected and slightly scandalous layer
jokes.explanation: A nice callback to the Roland/Marnie history. Marnie's knowing chuckle implies their past relationship is relevant to knowing 'how busy' Roland gets — the double meaning is clear without being crude. Good comedic economy.

jokes.text: Yeah, unfortunately I had to can his ass though, because of that whole 'cut the gravy' thing, so.
jokes.setup: Roland is explaining why he eventually 'let Johnny go' from his fake job
jokes.punchline: 'The cut the gravy thing' is a completely unexplained, absurd reference that Marnie apparently accepts without question — and we're left to imagine what it means
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best individual lines. 'The cut the gravy thing' is gloriously unexplained — its ambiguity is the joke. Is it a euphemism? A real incident? The show trusts the audience to find the mystery funnier than any explanation could be. Roland delivers it with complete confidence, as though it's obvious. High originality and quotability. This is the kind of absurdist detail that makes Schitt's Creek rewatchable.

jokes.text: Say something super mean. / What, I can't just turn it on in an instant, like you can.
jokes.setup: Alexis encourages Ted to get mad and say something mean as a kind of role play
jokes.punchline: Ted's 'like you can' — gently pointing out that Alexis has a talent for being mean — is a soft but real dig that reveals he's more perceptive than he lets on
jokes.explanation: A quiet, clean joke. Ted's 'like you can' is understated but pointed — he's acknowledged that Alexis has the capacity for cruelty without making it a fight. The character writing is good here.

jokes.text: Ted's escalating 'mean' things about the Rose family: David is 'a little pretentious,' Moira is 'a little uninformed'
jokes.setup: Alexis is encouraging Ted to let out his mean side in a 'role play'
jokes.punchline: Ted's version of 'mean' is so mild — 'a little pretentious,' 'a little uninformed at times' — that it's funnier than actual insults would be
jokes.explanation: Ted's 'mean' is the joke — his version of devastating criticism is so gentle it reads as a compliment. This is perfectly calibrated character comedy: we learn exactly who Ted is through what he considers an insult. Alexis's encouraging 'Yes! He is SO pretentious' amplifies the joke beautifully.

jokes.text: I find your brother can be a little pretentious sometimes. / Yes, yes! He is so pretentious, and it is so gross.
jokes.setup: Ted tries his first 'mean' thing
jokes.punchline: Alexis enthusiastically agrees that David is pretentious — completely missing that Ted was supposed to be being mean TO her, not about her family
jokes.explanation: The misdirection works well — Ted starts with David (the safe target), and Alexis gleefully piles on because David is fair game. Her 'it is SO gross' is a nice escalation. The setup for what's coming (Ted trying to criticize Alexis herself) is well constructed.

jokes.text: Well, and your mother... / Seems to be a little uninformed at times. / She's uninformed most of the time. / This is great, this is really good.
jokes.setup: Ted escalates to criticizing Moira; Alexis continues to enthusiastically agree
jokes.punchline: Alexis upgrades Ted's polite 'a little uninformed' to 'most of the time' — she's now co-writing the insults about her own family
jokes.explanation: The escalation of Alexis enthusiastically worsening Ted's polite criticisms of her own family is funny. Her 'This is great, this is really good' — coaching Ted in how to insult people — is delightfully self-unaware.

jokes.text: Well, and you, what about you? Are you just going to sit around all day, while I work and buy you dinners? You've gotta go out there and get a job... / Too far. / Mm-hmm. / It's too far, okay. / That's too far.
jokes.setup: Ted has been emboldened to criticize Alexis directly after success with David and Moira
jokes.punchline: The immediate simultaneous 'Too far' from both Ted AND Alexis — both realizing he crossed a line in the same beat — is a perfect comedic reverse
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured escalation payoff. The joke builds perfectly: Ted gets braver (David → Moira → Alexis), and the line he says about Alexis is the one that hits a real nerve. The fact that BOTH of them say 'too far' simultaneously is the best beat — Ted realizes he went too far AND Alexis agrees he went too far, which reveals a truth underneath the role play. Excellent structure and character integration.

jokes.text: One day, and that day may never come, I may call upon you to perform a function. / Marlon Brando! / Yes, but from what movie? / The Godfather. / No, it's the one where he's the big mafia guy. / The Godfather! / Hold on a second, I'll think of it.
jokes.setup: Roland quotes The Godfather to establish his terms with Johnny
jokes.punchline: Roland insists the movie is NOT The Godfather — 'the one where he's the big mafia guy' — despite being corrected twice
jokes.explanation: Roland's confident wrongness about The Godfather is a wonderful character joke. He's quoting the movie, acknowledging it's Marlon Brando, but insisting it's 'the one where he's the big mafia guy' — which IS The Godfather. His stubbornness in the face of two correct answers ('Hold on a second, I'll think of it') is both funny and perfectly in character. High quotability.

jokes.text: Why don't you just get me a little wiper fluid, and we'll call it even, okay? / Okay, wiper fluid, you got it. / And also maybe a Kleenex caddy? / I've always wanted one of those. / That's funny! / It is, but I'm not joking.
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to thank Roland for his help
jokes.punchline: Roland calls back the wiper fluid dispute from the truck scene, then escalates to a Kleenex caddy — and his 'It is, but I'm not joking' is perfect
jokes.explanation: Roland's callback to the wiper fluid is a clean structural payoff. But the real joke is the Kleenex caddy — it's such a specific and modest request from someone who just performed a significant act of friendship. 'I've always wanted one of those' is quietly revealing, and 'It is, but I'm not joking' is a perfect button that both acknowledges the absurdity and insists on it.

jokes.text: Roland, you screwed it up! Roland!
jokes.setup: Johnny has returned the borrowed car... in pieces, because the car broke down on the highway
jokes.punchline: Johnny blaming Roland as he arrives in Roland's truck — having just had two cars break down in one day — is a funny bit of misdirected frustration
jokes.explanation: A functional transition joke. Johnny's frustrated blaming of Roland for the car breakdown (which was his own fault for taking it on the highway) is a mild laugh. Not particularly well-developed but serves as a button to the day's misadventures.

jokes.text: Oh Johnny, I told you not to take 'er on the highway. / Yeah, I'm sorry, Bob, I guess I took one of those left turns, and uh, yeah.
jokes.setup: Bob sees his broken car being towed
jokes.punchline: Johnny's sheepish 'I guess I took one of those left turns' is a perfect callback to Bob's earlier specific warning about left turns leading to the highway
jokes.explanation: A strong callback payoff. Bob's specific 'no lefts' rule was established as a precise warning, and Johnny's 'I guess I took one of those left turns, and uh, yeah' is a perfectly embarrassed confession. The 'and uh, yeah' trailing off is well-observed. Rewatching with knowledge of the payoff makes the original warning funnier retroactively.

jokes.text: Okay, well, let's get 'er hooked up, get you back to the garage, and we'll start on the paperwork. / What paperwork? / It's all filled in, you just have to sign it, and uh... / She's all yours! / I'm not buying this car!
jokes.setup: Bob approaches the breakdown as a business opportunity
jokes.punchline: Bob has apparently pre-filled the sales paperwork — 'she's all yours!' — attempting to sell Johnny the car that just broke down twice
jokes.explanation: Bob's unashamed attempt to sell the broken-down car with pre-filled paperwork is a great small-town car dealer joke. The audacity of 'she's all yours!' after two breakdowns is funny because Bob either doesn't see why this is a problem or doesn't care.

jokes.text: I'll knock twenty bucks off. / You couldn't get twenty bucks for scrap! / We'll play a little hardball, okay. / Thirty bucks! / How about zero bucks?! / I wouldn't give you a dime for this! / Johnny, you're killing me here! / Fifty bucks!
jokes.setup: Johnny refuses to buy the broken car; Bob begins negotiating
jokes.punchline: Bob's negotiation goes UP (twenty → thirty → fifty bucks) as Johnny becomes MORE outraged — he's completely backwards
jokes.explanation: A beautifully absurd negotiation sequence. Normally prices go DOWN in negotiation; Bob's prices go UP as Johnny gets more hostile. 'Fifty bucks!' after 'How about zero bucks?!' is perfect absurdist escalation. 'You're killing me here!' as Johnny refuses is a great character beat. One of the episode's best sustained comedy sequences.

jokes.text: I'll throw in a couple of floor mats, but that's my final offer! / Walking!
jokes.setup: Johnny has declared he's walking; Bob calls after him
jokes.punchline: Bob offering floor mats as the closer on a car that's twice broken down is a perfect button — the specificity of 'floor mats' as his final desperate add-on
jokes.explanation: Floor mats as the final sweetener for a twice-broken car is a gloriously cheap and specific detail. The contrast between the car's uselessness and Bob's enthusiasm for the deal is perfectly absurd. 'But that's my final offer!' delivered at full conviction is excellent.

tmdb_overview: Moira and David try to earn money by selling cosmetics to the locals, while Johnny heads to Elmdale in hopes of collecting unemployment insurance.
insight: Character-driven cringe sustains a slower burn: 54 jokes in 26 minutes, mostly on David's social anxiety.
